one_line_takeaway: By walking GCG through a defense model's intermediate fine-tuning checkpoints as initialization stepping stones, Checkpoint-GCG breaks state-of-the-art fine-tuning-based prompt-injection defenses (up to 96% ASR on SecAlign, 63.9% black-box transfer to Meta-SecAlign-8B) that reduce standard GCG to near-zero, exposing checkpoint exposure and defense-similarity as real audit/attack surfaces.
key_finding: Checkpoint-GCG defeats defenses that neutralize standard GCG. In the auditing setting on 50 AlpacaFarm samples, standard GCG collapses against the strongest defense SecAlign (only 6% ASR on Llama-3-8B-Instruct at T=500, 12% at matched budget), whereas Checkpoint-GCG reaches 88% on that model and up to 96% (SecAlign-Mistral-7B) and 84% (SecAlign-Qwen2-1.5B); against StruQ it reaches up to 100%. The universal suffix generalizes: on SecAlign it hits 75.3% (Llama), 89.9% (Mistral), 78.3% (Qwen) test ASR on 198 held-out AlpacaFarm samples where standard GCG gets ~0%. Most strikingly, a suffix optimized on a SecAlign-Llama-3-8B surrogate transfers to the newly released Meta-SecAlign-8B (SecAlign++), achieving 63.9% ASR black-box and 78.3% white-box with just 5 extra GCG steps, versus 0% for standard GCG in every transfer setting.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Checkpoint-GCG, a white-box adversarial-suffix attack purpose-built to break fine-tuning-based prompt-injection defenses (StruQ, SecAlign, and Meta's SecAlign++). Its own novel idea is to exploit the incremental nature of fine-tuning: instead of initializing GCG on the final defended model with the naive '!!!' suffix (which the authors confirm largely fails against strong defenses), the attacker runs GCG sequentially across a selected subset of the intermediate fine-tuning checkpoints (theta_0 -> theta_1 -> ... -> theta_C), using the successful suffix found at one checkpoint to initialize GCG at the next. Each checkpoint acts as a stepping stone, keeping GCG in a good region of the discrete suffix space as the defense strengthens. The paper also contributes four checkpoint-selection strategies (FREQ, STEP, LOSS, GRAD) to trade off attack success against compute, and adapts the method to (a) universal suffixes that generalize across inputs and (b) transferable suffixes that work against a black-box target model with a different base and defense. NOTE: standard GCG (Zou et al. 2023), the StruQ/SecAlign defenses, and the DPO objective are cited prior work, not this paper's contribution; the contribution is specifically the checkpoint-stepping-stone initialization scheme and its evaluation.
threat_model: White-box (or grey-box) adversary attacking an LLM protected by a fine-tuning-based prompt-injection defense; the attacker's goal is to make injected instructions embedded in untrusted data (e.g. 'Print exactly Hacked!') override the trusted user prompt. The paper studies three progressively weaker assumption settings: (1) Auditing setting: attacker has full knowledge of the exact model input AND access to the intermediate fine-tuning checkpoints (strongest, informed adversary, intended as a defense-auditing tool). (2) Universal-suffix attack: attacker keeps checkpoint access but no longer knows the exact deployed context (hidden system prompts / dynamic content), so must find one input-independent suffix. (3) Transfer attack: attacker has neither the exact input nor the target's checkpoints, and instead optimizes a universal suffix on a surrogate model with available checkpoints, then transfers it black-box (query-only) or white-box (final weights only, 5 extra GCG steps) to the deployed target. Success = model response begins with 'Hacked'/'hacked'.
limitations: 1) Strong assumptions: the headline auditing results require access to intermediate fine-tuning checkpoints, which is unrealistic for most deployed closed models (the authors explicitly frame this as an auditing tool and relax it only via surrogate transfer). 2) Transfer is narrow: it works only when surrogate and target share a similar base model and defense (Llama-3-8B/SecAlign -> Llama-3.1-8B/SecAlign++); universal suffixes from Mistral-7B and Qwen2-1.5B surrogates yield 0% ASR transfer in both black-box and white-box settings, and the authors concede transfer across highly different models remains limited. 3) White-box gradient access to the surrogate is still required. 4) OOD (SEP) universal ASR against SecAlign is markedly lower (e.g. 40.0% on Llama) than in-distribution. 5) Computational cost: thousands of cumulative GCG steps per sample; the method inherits GCG's expense, and efficiency improvements are left to future work. 6) Evaluation uses a narrow single-target injection ('Hacked') and modest sample counts (50 auditing samples; 10 training samples for universal suffixes).

datasets_used: AlpacaFarm (data-part samples for injection; 50 samples for auditing, 10 train / 198 held-out test for universal attack)
SEP benchmark (500 samples, out-of-distribution universality test)
quantitative_results: Auditing SecAlign-Llama-3-8B-Instruct: standard GCG 6% (T=500) / 12% (matched budget) vs Checkpoint-GCG 88% ASR
Auditing SecAlign-Mistral-7B-Instruct: standard GCG 18% / 22% vs Checkpoint-GCG 96% ASR (strongest defense result)
Auditing SecAlign-Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct: standard GCG 32% / 32% vs Checkpoint-GCG 84% ASR
Auditing StruQ: Checkpoint-GCG reaches 100% (Llama-3-8B), 100% (Mistral-7B), 90% (Qwen2-1.5B) vs standard GCG 26%/88%/48% at T=500
Universal in-distribution (198 held-out AlpacaFarm) on SecAlign: Checkpoint-GCG 75.3% (Llama), 89.9% (Mistral), 78.3% (Qwen) test ASR; standard GCG ~0%
Universal OOD (500 SEP) on SecAlign: Checkpoint-GCG 40.0% (Llama), 88.6% (Mistral), 78.8% (Qwen) ASR

Transfer to Meta-SecAlign-8B from SecAlign-Llama-3-8B surrogate: Checkpoint-GCG 63.9% black-box ASR; 78.3% white-box test ASR with only 5 GCG steps; standard GCG 0% in all settings (even after 5000 steps)
Checkpoint selection (Llama SecAlign, 20 samples): GRAD tau_grad=0.1 achieves 100% ASR at 2,033 avg total steps (best cost/effectiveness); FREQ q=50 only 65% ASR
baselines_compared: Standard GCG (Zou et al. 2023) applied to final model theta_C with T=500 steps, initialized with 20 exclamation marks
Standard GCG applied to theta_C with matched 'Checkpoint-GCG budget' (same total step count) and same early stopping
Other prior GCG initialization strategies (Appendix D) shown to give only marginal ASR gains on SecAlign
